title: A nuclear clock based on $^{229}$Th
authors: Beichen Huang, Gaowei Yan, Qi Xiao, Wenhao Bu, Zhen Zhang, Chengchun Zhao, Chao Yan, Zhi-Ang Chen, Peixiong Zhang, Gleb Penyazkov, Zhenhai Zhan, Lingfeng Yan, Yuefei Wang, Lin Li, Shanming Li, Xiaobo Qian, Xuegang Liu, Qiange He, Taoxiang Sun, Haochen Tian, Binkun Lu, Ningyuan Ma, Juxian Li, Yanzhang Wu, Qiaorui Gong, Yuxiang Li, Haoyu Shi, Xiangliang Li, Longsheng Ma, Shining Zhu, Yuxiang Mo, Jun Lin, Li You, Yige Lin, Xibo Zhang, Yin Hang, Liangbi Su, Shiqian Ding
abstract: Atomic clocks have made time and frequency the most precisely measured quantities in physics, progressing from microwave standards that realize the SI second to optical clocks that now reach unprecedented levels of precision. A nuclear clock would shift the frequency reference from an electronic transition to the uniquely low-lying, laser-accessible isomeric transition in the $^{229}$Th nucleus, offering a route to compact, robust timekeeping and sensitive tests of fundamental physics. However, turning recent advances in spectroscopy of the $^{229}$Th nuclear resonance into clock operation requires the nuclear transition to serve as a stable discriminator for steering a traceable oscillator. Here we demonstrate the operation of a $^{229}$Th nuclear clock by stabilizing a continuous-wave narrow-linewidth 148.4 nm vacuum-ultraviolet (VUV) laser to a resolved nuclear transition in a solid-state host. This clock operation is enabled by fast frequency discrimination based on phototube photocurrent readout of the transmitted VUV power. The 10 $μ$W VUV laser, generated by four-wave mixing in cadmium vapour, provides a high-signal-to-noise absorption signal from a home-grown $^{229}$Th:CaF$_2$ crystal, allowing the laser to be locked to a weakly temperature-sensitive nuclear transition. The clock reaches a fractional frequency instability of $2\times10^{-12}/\sqrt{τ/s} $, where $τ$ is the averaging time. Remarkably, nuclear-clock frequencies measured with two distinct crystals agree at the $10^{-13}$ level, demonstrating the reproducibility of solid-state nuclear frequency references. By making a laser-addressed atomic nucleus an operational clock reference, this work extends quantum metrology from electronic to nuclear transitions, and opens a new platform for compact clocks, solid-state nuclear quantum sensors and precision tests of fundamental physics.
summary: The paper reports the successful demonstration of a novel nuclear clock based on the isomeric transition of $^{229}	ext{Th}$ within a solid-state crystal host. By stabilizing a VUV laser to this nuclear resonance, the authors achieve high frequency stability ($2 	imes 10^{-12}/\sqrt{	au} 	ext{ s}$). This work is significant because it extends quantum metrology into the realm of nuclear physics for next-generation compact timekeeping and fundamental constant measurements.
main_problem: To develop a robust, compact frequency reference by utilizing the low-lying isomeric transition in the $^{229}	ext{Th}$ nucleus instead of traditional electronic transitions.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated operation of a solid-state nuclear clock, achieving a fractional frequency instability of $2 	imes 10^{-12}/\sqrt{	au} 	ext{ s}$, and confirmed the reproducibility of this reference across different crystal batches.
method: Clock stabilization is achieved by locking a continuous-wave vacuum-ultraviolet (VUV) laser to the resolved nuclear transition using absorption spectroscopy read out via phototube photocurrent detection.
model_or_system: The system uses $^{229}	ext{Th}$ embedded in solid-state hosts, specifically $	ext{CaF}_2$ crystals ($	ext{S}1$ and $	ext{TS}1$). The interrogation relies on the laser-accessible isomeric transition frequency.
key_observables: Fractional frequency instability ($\Delta f/f$), absorption contrast, center frequencies of resolved nuclear transitions ($b$-line), and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).
important_parameters: VUV wavelength of $148.4 	ext{ nm}$; clock stability limit of $2 	imes 10^{-12}/\sqrt{	au} 	ext{ s}$; temperature control near $301	ext{ K}$.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the nuclear transition can serve as a stable, traceable discriminator for an external oscillator, and that high-SNR absorption measurements are feasible.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the complex experimental setup involving VUV laser generation (via four-wave mixing), optical frequency chain stabilization using frequency combs, and spectroscopic results comparing fluorescence and absorption signals across different crystal types.
paper_structure: The paper details the theoretical motivation for nuclear clocks, describes the advanced experimental apparatus required to generate and stabilize the necessary VUV light, presents measurements characterizing the spectral lines in solid-state hosts, and culminates in demonstrating clock operation and stability analysis.
why_interesting: This work directly advances quantum metrology by extending high-precision timing standards from electronic transitions (like those used in optical clocks) to nuclear energy levels, opening a new platform for fundamental physics tests using solid-state systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Experimental platform for a solid-state 229Th nuclear clock. a, Experimental setup. Continuous-wave 148.4 nm VUV light is generated by four-wave mixing in cadmium vapour and sent to a home-grown 229Th:CaF2 crystal. Nuclear fluorescence is collected with a photomultiplier tube (PMT), while the transmitted VUV power is recorded with a phototube or a PMT. During absorption spectroscopy and clock operation, the 710 nm laser is modulated, and the transmitted VUV signal is detected via lock-in demodulation. b, Cadmium energy-level scheme for resonantly enhanced four-wave mixing. Two 375 nm photons drive the two-photon resonance between the 5 1S0 and 6 1S0 states, while one 710 nm photon...

Fig. 2. Fluorescence and absorption spectroscopies of 229Th:CaF2 crystals. a, Overview of the observed nuclear transitions on an absolute VUV-frequency axis. Fitted line profiles of the D-centre quadrupole components and the O-centre feature are shown, with peak heights normalized to line b. b, Normalized fluorescence-excitation spectra of line b, the O-centre transition and line c. All error bars represent 1σ statistical uncertainties. c, Frequency-modulation absorption signals of the same three spectral features measured with an analog PMT in S1. Each data point is acquired for 80 seconds. d, Corresponding absorption signals measured with the phototube in S1. Each data point is acquired...

Fig. 3. Fractional frequency instability of the nu- clear clock. Fractional frequency instabilities under three configurations: the S1 crystal with signal measured by PMT (triangle), the S1 crystal with signal measured by phototube (square), the TS1 crystal with signal measured by phototube (circle). To better show the dependence of the frequency in- stabilities on the averaging time τ, a dashed line is plotted for each configuration, with a slope corresponding to 1/√τ.
